title: This Startup Fused Human Brain Cells with Silicon Chips | E2295

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: If you ever heard terms like MVP, minimum viable product, or lean startup...

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: the world's first biological data center. When they compared it against their reinforcement learning systems, the neurons we had were 5,000 times more stable efficient.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Has anyone complained that you're tinkering a bit with the edges of humanity?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: The Vatican were worried about this. You do not want to create conscious systems because ethically a conscious system has the ability to suffer and we do not want any suffering to come about from any technology.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Hello and welcome back to TWiST. Now today we're talking to a company we spoke to in 2022 because I thought they were one of the most interesting startups in the entire world. Called Cortical Labs, they are trying to fuse silicon chips that we all know and love with human neurons, bringing the biological and the synthetic together to create a new type of computer, a biological computer. I was so tickled by the idea I had to talk to them, but since that first conversation, Cortical Labs has built out data centers of its biological computers, which means that we now have real robust capacity to bring humans and computers together. So, to tell us more about what's been going on over in the realm of Cortical Labs, please welcome back to the show, it's my dear friend Hon. Hon, how are you doing?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Good, thanks. It's great to chat again, Alex. And congratulations, I heard you had a new baby.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yes, that's why I've been extra tired these last four months, but we're powering through through the just the grace of coffee. All right, so Hon, last time we talked, you had just put out your CL1, which was the first kind of like fully contained biological computer with neurons and chips and you were selling them, I think, for something like $35,000 a piece. So before we get deep into the tech, just for the business folks out there, how has that product performed in market?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: We've kind of exhausted our entire stock of 30 units that we had capped. Um, so that's good and you can work out how much that ended up becoming.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Best part of a million.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and, uh, we have actually, so I'm in the US right now partly because A, we're fundraising, but at the same time, I've also, you know, CEO stands for Chief Everything Officer, right? I'm also now the company courier. I just dropped off a unit at Johns Hopkins, uh, with some of the folks there. Uh, I just came from Boston, so Mass General got a unit, another one just got dropped off at UCSF. So now there is about five US institutions with a CL1 device. The other one being Dartmouth, they got an early preview early last year.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So tell folks what the CL1 is and maybe because you have one with you, show us a little bit for folks on the video version what it looks like.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, absolutely. So maybe before I show it, like to give a very brief summary, the CL1 is our attempt to build a computing platform that allows researchers and developers to get going with biological computing very easily. Uh, it saves you the effort of building your own hardware, writing your own software, and you just program these things with Python and, you know, they're rack mountable 3U unit systems. Um, I have one here to show. So excuse me, if the video is a bit...

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: bit jerky and this is a CL1. Actually, maybe from the front here you can see what they look like from the front.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It looks like a very long space-age toaster.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yes, so they're actually very similar to rack-mountable sleds that some of the GPUs come in the same form factor. So this is 3U, so in a server rack cabinet, this will take up about three rows. In a 45U system, we can pack 20 of these units. And we have about 120 so far in six racks in our lab in Australia that we're calling the world's first biological data center. You know, somebody came by and they're like, 'Hey, don't you sell biological computers?' And I was like, 'Yeah.' And they're like, 'Well, what do you call a place with a lot of computers?' I don't know, 'data center?' They're like, 'Well, now you've got a biological data center.' I was like, 'Huh, that's really nifty.'

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: But I'm just going to show you what it actually looks like. So we open up the top, this is the neural chamber. So this is where we load up the compute unit. So neurons go into this chip. There's a life support system that is connected to it. There's a heating element underneath here that keeps it at a nice 37 degrees Celsius, at around 100 Fahrenheit. We close this latch, and think of this as our Neuralink, right? So this is the neural interface. There's a compute unit at the back, and then all of this stuff here is life support. So we build mechanisms to keep the system flowing, to give nutrients to the brain, to remove the waste. So we have pumps here like the heart, we have a feeding and a waste reservoir, so kind of think of it like a stomach and a bladder. These are filtration units like the kidney. We have a gas mixer like the lungs...

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: and also the really interesting thing is you also have your traditional and non-traditional IO units. So we look at the back here, this is USB-C, USB-A, Ethernet, but you also have gas inputs. So filtered room air, CO2, nitrogen, and we have a waste gas outlet here to relieve the pressure. We jokingly call it the fart valve.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I'm just laughing because we're talking about, you know, rack mounting and how much space it takes up in a server rack, and then you're like, 'And here are the lungs, and here's where it expels waste.' It's such an interesting combination of things that I understand but never see together.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Now, how many neurons can you pack into a CL1, and then in compute-friendly terms, how much power is that that you bring to bear?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so in a CL1, you can go all the way up to a million, two million neurons if you so wish. People grow organoids on these things and they have several million neurons. For the cortical cloud and the offerings that we have, we go down to about 200,000 neurons, A, because that's a number that, you know, we can grow pretty easily and keep them alive.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: quite well, uh, makes it commercially viable, but also we've found that you can actually get some learning and training with that. So—

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So, I want to make an analogy here. Because I think that everyone's very familiar with the idea of parameters in an—in an LLM. You know, like, um, 105B would be 105 billion parameters. If it's mixture of experts, yes, fewer than 105 billion would be activated, blah, blah, blah, but we kind of get that number. So, 200,000 neurons, a million neurons, same number to me. I have no idea if that's a lot, not very many, so, how many neurons do I have upstairs? And, um, how many do you need to actually have the CL1 function as a biological computer?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I can't remember exactly the number, but I think it's like 100 billion neurons that you have in—in your brain. So, you know, we have that, and then a ton more, like trillions of synapses. Um, so, the closest thing that, you know, you can analogize what we have here is maybe a cockroach. Or, or, you know, a fly kind of thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So, that actually doesn't—tell me why I'm wrong here, Hon, but, um, cockroach is not famous for their intelligence. Famous for their durability, but I mean, not—not being super smart. But when you combine that number of neurons with a—a—a chip, a silicon chip, um, what—what's like the—the multiplication factor that we get from bringing those two things together?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yep. Actually, so cockroaches are actually pretty smart. Uh, along with apologies with bees and flies. You—you—you know, you—you're not wrong in some cases that they're not intelligent in the way we view like human intelligence. Like, they're not going to solve calculus, right? But what they do solve really well is, have you ever tried killing a fly? They're really hard to kill.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So hard! They're so quick.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: They're so quick, and they're so agile, they are—they almost like predict your actions ahead of time. Um, and so, you know, this whole thing, and I think, you know, the industry needs to get this terminology right. We have the super intelligence. Like, you know, GPT-whatever, 5.5 is super intelligent. Is it generally intelligent? No, right? Because, you know, the—what is it? Steve Wozniak has the best, like, test for AGI. Can you walk into a stranger's kitchen and make yourself a cup of coffee? Everything is different, everything is new, you'll have to experiment with it, right? You've never seen it before. We don't have that yet. So, I would say that, um, biology, um, even very simple organisms like, you know, a fly has generalized intelligence... some people not machine has. Um, so, uh, you know, we're hoping to exploit those properties in even very simple systems, and you know, maybe we can get a lot of stuff done there without having to go into the realms of, you know, human intelligence and all the baggage that comes with it, like consciousness and so forth.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So, when I—when I think about biological computing, I presume we're talking about a lot of use cases in drug testing and drug discovery, you know, biosimilars, blah, blah, blah, all the stuff that's kind of flesh and blood. But do you foresee a future in which the probably not the CL1, maybe the CL3, uh, manages to bring sil—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: ...silicon and neurons together in a way that creates a computer that is better than today's GPUs and TPUs and so forth at certain types of calculations that we use in the technology world versus the biology world?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Founder scale faster on Deel. That's the deal. You can grow your company without borders and you can set up payroll for any country in minutes. Hire anyone anywhere like a modern startup or large company does. And Deel is going to get all the visas handled fast so you can get back to building. full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, actually there is one that is already like proven to be better than CPUs or GPUs and that's in reinforcement learning. So this is some very novel work, it's still getting written up so I don't really want to jinx it, but we're hoping to get it published later this year at NeurIPS... which you know hopefully is also happening down in my part of the world in in Sydney. And, you know, what we've discovered is doing some work with a bit of a really strong research partner, the question was, can these neurons exhibit goal-seeking behavior or pathfinding, right? And the answer is yes, but not only was the answer yes, which you know shocked us, the secondary thing that they discovered was when they compared it against their reinforcement learning systems, their benchmark the GPU-based systems, the neurons we had were 5,000 times more cellular efficient than their GPU-based systems. What that means is that for every step a biological system is doing, it takes 5,000 times, 5,000 steps more to do on a GPU system. The saving grace with GPU reinforcement learning is that you can just accelerate time, so you just run time 5,000 times faster than the real world.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: The caveat with that is you can't accelerate time if you're a robot. You're operating at the same speed like everyone else in the physical world.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So if biological computing is good at reinforcement learning, which as I think everyone listening to this knows is an enormous part of improving AI models today, you could end up pretty far outside of the realm of the biology side of industry. That's very interesting. But I feel like I've taken us down the wrong path. Let's back up and talk about—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: ...what you guys have built. So you've built a biological data center, you have 120 units in Melbourne, and you're going to do one in Singapore next, which I believe can get even bigger?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yes, exactly. So we're working with a data center company called Day1. Um, you know, they've partnered with us because A, they liked the technology for several reasons. A, because it is um an alternative way to do compute that doesn't affect their energy budget. So this way they can say, you know, we have a data center operating at a very tight window, specified by the Singapore government of 200 megawatts. They're going to provide the same chips like everyone else, but on top of that, they're going to provide our compute, which is not affecting any of the energy budget because they don't have to do any special cooling. And one unit only uses about 30 watts of energy, so a rack is—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Which is basically zero for this conversation.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Correct. So, you know, they're getting more for not much, you know, in terms of costs. So um that is one of the the reasons why they've partnered with us. But secondarily as well, and I think this is something that is a little bit mind-breaking as well, they've built not just the space to house a thousand CR1 units, but next to it a laboratory for us to grow the cells for the compute on site.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, so you don't have to ship in your stem cell-based, I point out we're not killing people here to steal their brains, stem cell-based neurons, you can just make them, grow them, no?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Raise them? Make them, grow them, same thing, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay. I'm sorry, it's my usual words don't work as well when we translate them over to here. I'm sure you've already gone through this, but for me it's still novel. Um, so what does that save in terms of like operating costs for you guys to not have to stick them in a cooler and fly them?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Um, a tremendous amount. But it also means that there is no supply chain constraint. It it it decouples your data center from not just being a place where you just buy from a central vendor and you wait for it to come in and you, you know, you deploy your chips, to one where you're also manufacturing your chips on site. So you kind of decentralize the entire model where, you know, every data center is self-sufficient. Every data center technically is not reliant on one vendor from one country.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Remind me how long the neurons live before they need to be replaced?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Um, so neurons can actually live a very long time if you keep them um in a, you know, well-kept, so to speak. Um, the the thing that does require replenishing are the tube sets. And the main the main culprit for that is um are these filtration units here, let me see.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: If you're on the audio version, he's pointing at—

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: I'm pointing at these cartridges here. Think of them a bit like kidneys and what happens is that over time, um these filtration units um clog up with large protein growth factors and they kind of result in a bit like a kidney failure situation. So yeah, we just swap them out and then we go.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: ...in another four to six months.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Okay, so, so really then, it's, uh, what, what do you feed the neurons? I was about to say sugar water as a joke, but I think it might actually be a little bit right.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: No, it actually is pretty much sugar water.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So basically, you've made a, a biological system that keeps a small number, compared to our brains, of neurons alive that are connected to these chips that do a lot of cool things.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Here's my question though. As time goes on, do you think that as chips get smarter, we're going to need more neurons to interface with them? Or is this amount of biological compute on top of a chip enough, and we'll get gains just from improving the chip component of this? I'm trying to kind of figure out, like, putting some dots on the chart for where we're going.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so it's always a case of, like, push and pull, right? So for instance, um, if, if let's say we referred back to GPUs, right? We had way more GPUs than what we knew what to do with them before we got to the LLMs. Um, and the, the breakthrough was the algorithm, um, which, you know, was a breakthrough success and we were like, oh, we need more, more processing power.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: And so there's always this, like, push and pull tension between, are the algorithms there yet? Or are the, you know, is it a hardware limitation? Right now, we, we see this as an algorithm's limitation, because the bottleneck is how do you, how do you best represent digital information that is all on the internet and so forth, with analog systems, right, which is you and I?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: So, um, that, that I think is, is still being worked on. You know, you have really smart people in Neuralink and Synchron working on that for the BCI side.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: We're kind of working on it as well, but we have an additional challenge, which we have to write information directly into the neurons. So, um, you know, to, to answer your question, we, we don't think we've saturated the system yet, so we have enough capacity with our current setup. But who knows? Maybe somebody cracks another new algorithm and then we're like, okay, we gotta get more cells going on the system.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I'm just really excited about what you're working on, because when we think about the systems we're using to make artificial intelligence smarter, we're throwing the equivalent of bodies at it. We're throwing more GPUs, the Bitter Lesson, Jevons Paradox, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: But we're working on systems that are so fundamentally much more power-hungry, less efficient, and less attuned to the problems we're trying to solve than our brains, the things we already have with us. And so to me, it, it just seems very logical that as the chips get better and as we make, get better algorithms to make the neurons function as we need them to, we should be able to have two different intelligence curves working in, in, in synchronized fashion and we should get much faster gains.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And this is when religion comes into it.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So, uh, as background, Hon, I was raised, uh, in a very conservative Christian church. And so growing up in the 90s, I heard a lot about stem cells and cloning, and a lot of just what I would call fear-mongering. I'm now a non-religious science fiction nerd. So I'm pretty much your biggest fan.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: But I'm a little worried about how we're going to tell people that we're fusing neurons with computers because I think you've now taken this from proof of concept when you guys played Pong to early commercial with the CL1 to playing Doom recently. That was cool. To now building out data centers in an international format. So this is coming to market. Now we can talk about this sort of thing. Has anyone, the Pope or similar, complained that you're tinkering a bit with the edges of humanity and is anyone worried?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Actually, the Vatican were worried about this, but fortunately, Brent, my CSO, has done an excellent job, you know, engaging with bioethicists and actually, you know, being at the forefront of this, right? And so I think, you know, because of the space that we're working in and the fact that, you know, there's a lot of ethics that need to go into it. Even just doing research work with, you know, any biomedical aspect to it requires an ethics board. We're very attuned to these kinds of potential criticisms, right? So we try to engage them proactively.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: If you're growing a startup or a small business, you can't sleep on incoming calls, even if they come in after hours or on the weekends. That's some of your best customers calling you when they need you. That's why today's episode is brought to you by Quo, Q-U-O. The smarter way to run your business communications. Quo is the number one top-rated business phone system on G2 and it's trusted by more than 90,000 businesses. They keep releasing new features that I didn't even know I needed. Money is on the line, always say hello with Quo. Try Quo for free plus get 20% off your first six months when you go to quo.com/twist. That's Q-U-O.com/twist.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: And I think, you know, firstly, the most important thing that we're trying to work on right now is to get everybody in this space plus the consciousness space to, you know, come together and actually agree on a shared nomenclature, right? Because if you cannot agree on the same thing that we are all studying on, then we have no chance of actually making any progress and all we get is just a lot of fear with not much understanding. So we're trying to do that. And also, yeah, I think it was actually Brett was in discussion with the Vatican who wanted to find out, you know, what's going on here and was there any religious and ethical, like, issues. And, you know, fortunately, they actually agreed that what we were doing was alright and it was actually, you know, fine. Yeah. Because because ultimately, the main question that we all we need to have is, you know, is there a harmonization part of the plan and also...

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: You know, the principle of double doctrine is there actually a net good that can be come from this technology versus a net negative. And so that's the reason why, you know, you started out with the traditional drug discovery, disease modeling and stuff because that's still a very important part of the work.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: That is the primary use case for all these labs purchasing these devices which is, you know, so, you know, in Mass General, it's a dementia- Alzheimer's dementia researcher, at Hopkins, they're looking at toxicology and alternatives to animal testing, at UCSF, they're looking at, I think they're looking at movement disorders and a whole bunch of other like really-

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, that's nice. Yeah. These are all by the way, this is why this technology even in its current form is freaking awesome. You just listed three major medical centers in the US that are using this technology now to make our lives better.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So like this- the dangers that are hypothetical down the road that we're touching on are not meant to undercut the current usefulness and commercial application and research application of this product. I'm just having fun. Keep going.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, thank you. And so, you know, that- that- that is- it's all happening right now. The compute side of it is by all means the smallest but the newest, the one that's also most fraught with risk because you never know what people are going to build. So this is something that we're trying to like keep a hold of.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: And I think internally at the company this is really important to understand this whole this discussion of consciousness because I think we've drawn a red line for us which you do not cross which is- where is this line and we gotta figure out where this is, is consciousness.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: You do not want to create conscious systems because ethically a conscious system has the ability to suffer and we do not want any suffering to come about from any technology. So that is the stance that we take.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: We try to do as much as we can when we have the cloud, we can monitor things, but you know once these things go out of the, you know, into the into the real world, we don't really know what's going on. But you know fortunately most of our users actually 90% of all all our purchases have all come from academic R&D institutions that you know we trust that they they do the right thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: But the- the cloud that you guys have built, the data center in Melbourne or Melbourne or whatever, and then Singapore, um, you know, we're going to have if it's 200,000 neurons a piece and we're going to have I think it's up to a thousand in Singapore, you can do the math, it's quite a lot.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Um, the reason why it doesn't scare me is it's all kind of like cut up into little pieces like each one has X number of neurons, but there is the science fiction you know question of if you have a data center full of these and they can interact, does that change the math?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: But the good news is that I think we're pretty far away from having so many biological computers in the world all plugged in together that we have to think about that. So getting back to practical applications here, why go the cloud route as opposed to just selling the devices? Because clearly if you sold out your first run, plenty of demand.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Um, why the- yeah, so cloud. I'm just curious about why that was the right commercial approach to the market.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Ultimately what we really want to do at Cortical Labs is to, you know, hate the word democratize, but it is democratize the technology by by reducing the accessibility barrier to biological computing, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Because, you know, ultimately, I always go back to NVIDIA, right? Because ultimately, the AI that we have today is actually an accident. It was completely a fluke that it happened, right? Because, you know, everyone's like, yeah, Jensen was so smart, he's so brilliant about this. I was like, yeah, if he was so brilliant, why did it take seven years from when CUDA was made to when Alex Krizhevsky made AlexNet, right? It was a sheer serendipitous moment where somebody was Jeff Hinton's grad student who was solving for image recognition technology who had a GPU and you had a program CUDA.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I like to think we would have had some other serendipitous meeting point along the way, but that is, you know, how it happened. And it does feel a little tenuous, a little fragile. And you look back, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Exactly, right. And so, you know, what what Jensen did, you know, I think brilliantly at the start was he made CUDA free. He made it so that any GPU, even the the crappiest gaming GPU, could also run CUDA. And so the accessibility barrier was really low, so anyone could get into it. So that's what we're trying to do here with with the cloud system, because, you know, unfortunately if you have a DishBrain, you really can't do anything with this unless you have a lab and you can grow cells and you, um, you know, can keep them alive.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right, so you need to have a person who's doing the kidney replacement and the feeding and the waste extraction and so forth.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Exactly, exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Got it.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: But what if you don't have any of that, but you have a great idea you want to experiment with? So the idea of the cloud was born, where we said, let's just give it to people with ideas so they can muck around with it. And that's actually the story of Doom. We actually didn't build Doom. The Doom was actually a student developer who participated in a hackathon at Stanford, no biology background, used our API SDK, and he built something that was really cool. And so we decided to, you know, help him tell the story and word got around and I think he's now been accepted into a very prestigious incubator program. I shall now not name which.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Uh, could it—could it be called, uh, Kai-Combinator? Or rhyme with that?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Can neither confirm nor deny.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Got it, got it. So, so last time we talked, Hon, uh, we were talking about the first application of your technology to a video game which was Pong. Uh, and for the young people, Pong is a game when you have two paddles on either side of the screen, they go up and down and knock a ball back and forth. Yes, we used to think that was fun. It still is, frankly. Um, but you told me that when you—when you built the system, you had to kind of like create a reward or punishment mechanism—I forget which—to give it the incentives to learn how to play correctly, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Correct, yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: In Pong, not a lot of variables. In Doom, many more. So do you know how they managed to set up the reward or punishment mechanisms to actually interact with a game of that complexity? Because I'm curious if it was hard or not.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So, uh, pretty much it's also the same. They're using auditory stimulus versus...

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: ...disordered stimulus as the reward and punishment signal, um, there were a few more variables and, and you know, honestly it's kind of funny because I think, um, they didn't, they didn't disincentivize or punish it for wasting ammo because there was no ammo restrictions. And so essentially what it did was it figured out that if it just spammed the shoot button and just spin around in a circle, it would just win. So, um, we, it's kind of funny because that's what happens in actual reinforcement learning systems as well. So when we changed that, um, you know, it actually was punished for wasting ammo and all that stuff, and it started to learn and actually started to have some interesting gameplay from that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I just realized why it's very important that you don't create conscious systems because they can feel pain, because if you're using disordered inputs as a punishment mechanism, it's a little bit harsh, everybody, we're working on the terms. But you wouldn't want to do that to a conscious system.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Correct, exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, okay. That makes, I've connected that and that makes a lot more sense. But here's the takeaway. I mean, okay, look, Doom's great. If you haven't played Doom 3, do it. You owe yourself to have that fun. Um, but if a kid, and I say that with love, kid, can build this with your API, to me it shows that the technology is not that hard to bring to real-world application, hence the cloud. And that brings me to my last question, which is, how many people, how many customers can you serve with 120 CL-1s in a data center? One? 10,000? I literally have no idea what the range is.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: So, so, um, we have 120 that we've deployed. Unfortunately, you know, there has been some teething with the biology, so while we do have 120, only about 20 or 30 users are on the cloud at the moment. Actually, there are 20 users. We have another 10 more that we have to bring online, but that's also because, you know, there's a whole conveyor belt-like system of getting cells grown and all that stuff. Um, and it's one of those things where unfortunately, because it takes some time for it to grow...

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: What we see now is the result of decisions made two months ago kind of thing. So they only starting to come online, we've had a slow sort of start. But yeah, a lot of... sorry, no, people have started to come on board and the waiting queue is getting smaller. Um, so yeah, we, you know, we have that and we actually have a couple of corporates and partners coming on board to do some experimental work.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: But, so, when Doom is running on your biological computers, is that one CL1? Is that five? Like, how much does it take?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: One. It was just one. So we haven't come up with the chaining of the systems yet. That's coming in I think two or three...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Oh okay, so each one's still a discrete box. They're not... okay, that... then my question was actually relatively silly, then you could serve 120 customers at once.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, exactly. I mean, there... that's fine, because, you know, this is what we're doing at the moment. There has been some internal research work about, you know, where we... we're doing PDMS microfluidic wells so we can actually segment out the surface of the chip and you know, rather than you get all 60, maybe you only need 16. And that way, I don't know if you're familiar with like VMware, maybe you can get a hyper-v... like a virtual machine with multiple people running the same thing. So that way we can increase yield and stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So PDMS is a polydimethylsiloxane, yep, which is also known as a dimethicone. What... what are we talking about?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: It's a material. It's like a gel-type of material that you can make microfluidic devices with. Um, so think of it like a... like a well. Actually, I'll show you what it looks like. So...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Show me. Because and then define microfluidics for me because again, I nod my head like I understand, but we are outside of my comfort zone. This is not SaaS economics, so you're going to have to help me along.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Okay, so I'm going to show you this. So this is a microfluidic device. So there are probably several thousand neurons in one well, um, and they are now segmented into these little sections and we actually have channels that we can turn on and turn off. Um, so we can actually control this more like a circuit now.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And what we're looking at here, if you didn't know what it was, you would think these were top-down views of water storage tanks with pumps and pipes coming into them. But instead, each one of these little octagons...

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Octagons.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Each one of these octagons has a bunch of neurons in it, and the microfluidics allows you to feed and then also transfer information between them?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, the microfluidics are these channels and what... what they do is they constrain the growth of the axon, the long part of the neurons. So they can only communicate with their nearest neighbors or we can, you know, block them off and say you can only communicate with your east-west neighbor or your north-south... So you have a lot more like circuitry-like control over that.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right, and then you could in theory segregate to have more than one person using the same computer because you can essentially not let the axons get too busy.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Sharing information, got it.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Right. So this is kind of like a chiplet model that we've been experimenting with. So we can say, alright, you can have this one here, you'll have one, two, three, three, four, four electrodes to work from and if your workload is pretty low, maybe this is just good enough and, you know, you can actually have if you want get two and then you can have them sort of network up like this and then we can, you know, share the instance and stuff like that. So there's a lot of things that we're working on internally to try to figure out, hey, can we increase yield, can we get more interesting like, you know, learning some of that kind of stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So how much are you raising? What's your target?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, we're raising... Oh, and actually one more thing. We're raising 30, but I also wanted to show you guys this. So, so this is the Cortical Cloud.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Ah. Yeah, if you're on the audio version, it looks like a standard developer backend, which is a compliment.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yes. And then you just hit the, the instance that you've acquired. Um, and there you go. I'm in New York right now, my lab is in Melbourne, and I am live streaming neural activity from a cell culture that my team have assigned me for demo purposes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What is it doing? What are these dots that I'm seeing?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: If you're on the audio version, imagine, uh, you're looking out the window of a spaceship and you're seeing stars go by, that's what it looks like in slow-mo.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yep. So, the, the window with stars, this is what we call raster plot. So as we go down you can see the channels are like increasing in number and if you look on the right side, that's the topological mapping of it. So that's the surface of the chip. And so, every time there's a, a spike or an action potential, we're marking it on this, um, grid here. And we're also marking on this. So you can actually see there's like a temporal, um, correlation, you can see bursting activity, you can see synchrony on this view so like you can see this cluster here, there's another cluster there and, um, you know, after a while you can watch this... Oh look, they're getting really active here. Um, you can just watch this and sort of like figure out what's going on. Um, and then you can also look at the activity views, so here we can actually see the raw waveform. So this is kind of the same thing that a Neuralink would be picking up and, you know, if we wanted to like, you know, give them a bit of a poke, we can just say, alright, I'm going to poke the neurons in this region here and I can click and I've delivered a stimulus across and then that wakes them up a bit, um, going on.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: It's really good it's not conscious, otherwise that would be kind of rude like 'I was sleeping!'

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: I know, yeah, how rude. Now, had a good dream there. We can see like the little spike waveforms, we can actually see the, you know, what the shape of them are, so you can verify that they're actually really like, um, action potential spikes. Um, and the, the cool thing here is that if you're a developer, by the way, I'm on Team Human here so we still need developers to think of really cool applications, because LLMs are not going to solve this for us. Um, you write all the application code in, in Python and, um, yeah they're all in Jupyter Notebooks, so you, you specify the what programmers actually do with CL1 is, uh, think of them as mini architects for mini matrices, right? So you're building...

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: The matrix and the neurons are going to be put in. You specify the parameters of the game, the reward and punishments, the objective functions and so forth, and you just let them, you know, get connected to it. And so yeah, this is how you can do it. Documentation's on docs.corticallabs.com. There's an SDK you can pip install and off you go.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: You know, it's one of the best parts about talking to founders that I get to do on this is just straight up getting to see the future. Usually it feels like I'm looking about 12 feet out. This is one of those chats which I feel like I'm looking 12 years out. Like there's enough optimization, expansion, programming languages, how to teach these things, how... like there's so much here that isn't done yet, you know? I feel like you have like... well, you have your life's work ahead of you. This is going to be... this is going to be a lot.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah.

full_transcript.text: Yes, so check us out at corticallabs.com. The cloud is at cloud.corticallabs.com. Documentation, docs.corticallabs.com. I'm on Twitter as well, DR1337. And also, we are looking for talented developers. Stay tuned to our Twitter feed. There may be a hackathon coming up at MIT soon.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Are you going to go to that?

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Yeah, probably will have to go for that one.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Absolutely. Yeah, for sure. Yeah.
