full_transcript.text: All right. So stay tuned and yeah, more developers, channeling the inner Steve Ballmer: Developers, developers, developers, developers! At least you're not wearing a sweaty collared shirt. Hon, thank you so much for coming back to the show. We'll see you again soon.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Thank you.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: We're talking about drones. No, not drones that sit on the water or go beneath the water or just fly up in the sky carrying a single hand grenade. No, today we're going to talk about very large drones, mostly outside of the battlefield context. So I rang up my dear friend Michael Norcia from Pyka to come on and tell us about why drones are good for agriculture, why they're good for near-term cargo transport, and why they may soon be dropping couches from the sky onto your head as long as you're within a radius of 50 meters. Please welcome to the show, it's Michael from Pyka. How you doing?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Awesome. Thank you for having me. Doing well.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So I'm really excited about your company because I thought of you guys as the company that makes large drones that are powered by batteries that sprays crops. But as it turns out, you guys have really broadened out in the last couple of years and do quite a lot more. But Michael, to get to that point, I feel like we should go back to the future. So take me back to the start of the company, the initial vision and how you guys literally quite got off the ground.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, great question. So honestly, the start of the story begins when I was like four years old. I've been just incredibly passionate about aviation my entire life. So I started, you know, with paper airplanes, glider...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: I was about 4, rubber band powered airplanes, little electric planes, pretty much obsessively built aircraft throughout my childhood. Ended up studying physics and then I got to work on a number of different eVTOL projects in the Bay Area, three or four of them actually, so mostly passenger carrying vertical takeoff and landing air taxi concepts. Yep. Super fun, like, you know, really hard technical challenges. But it was clear this was like almost 10 years ago, there was just no way we were going to certify those aircraft for commercial use for another, like, realistically two decades. And that took the wind out my sails, so decided to leave to start Pyka and yeah, that was, that was now almost 10 years ago, so it's a long journey.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: When was the first flight and how far have you guys gone in terms of, like, commercial penetration of the agricultural sector with your drone that can handle crop spraying? 'Cause I'm curious if you've managed to gain a material market share in that industry.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So first flight was, uh, actually like a week before Demo Day. We went through Y Combinator. And so we, it was a wild time. Uh, we spent one month designing a sort of 600-pound, what would be about a two-passenger autonomous aircraft. Uh, we built it in two months, actually a little under two months, and flew it on like week 11 of the whole program a week before Demo Day. Uh, so that was awesome. And it actually flew itself, it took off and landed running our own software. Um, you know, great testament to the realities of hardware. Like, we had a flying prototype autonomous big UAV 11 weeks in. That was, I think, literally like 1% of the work that we've done now. So the other 99% has been going from, you know, prototype to now a, like, widely deployed aircraft that is operating with customers literally seven hours, sorry, seven days per week. Uh, you know, some uh, customers are doing 12 to 13 hour shifts. Um, so yeah, where we are today, um, believe it or not, we're actually the only company on earth that I'm aware of that has deployed a Group 4 UAS to commercial customers at scale. And interestingly, this is happening in the like rural jungles of Brazil. Um, that is the only place on earth that big drones like this are being flown commercially.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That's weird. Talk to me about Group 4. I don't think that's a particularly well-known metric outside of the UAS space.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so uh, it's a more like military classification of drones. It's basically drones that are bigger than 1,320 pounds. Um, so you know, things like the MQ-9 Reaper, that's the most common Group 4 UAS.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right. Now, you said you'd only done 1% of the work when you'd gone from no plane or no big drone to having a big drone that can fly. A lot of people out there that watch this or listen to this are software founders, and they're probably familiar with if you code up the feature, that does not mean product-market fit, it doesn't mean the sales cycle is over. There's still a lot of work to be done once you have some code. But for folks outside the hardware space, tell them why it was...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's only 1% of the work because from the outside going from no plane to plane sounds like you made a lot of progress.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, totally. So, I mean the thing with our products are a blend of hardware and software. And so really like the last nine years have been this just steady march of maturing both the hardware and software in parallel. The hardware I think is the particularly difficult one to mature quickly. So that first aircraft, you know, couldn't actually complete an interesting customer mission. It didn't have the features it needed to spray a crop, it couldn't really move cargo even. So there was a lot of like work there. But, you know, I think the most interesting thing really is actually what's been happening in the last three or four years for us with commercial deployments with customers. So, you know, a prototype can be operated by n-number of engineers, it can be operated by your entire company if need be. You know, your uptime requirements are non-existent. If you fly for one hour in one week, you'll be like, hell yeah, we got an awesome video, that's all we need.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Put it on YouTube immediately we're raising the next round.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Exactly. With customers it's completely different. You know, our customers are very, very upset if the aircraft is down for more than 24 hours. And these customers exist in like extremely remote regions of the world. Like they are literally in some cases a six-hour drive from the nearest city. Right. And so getting to that sort of level of uptime takes an extraordinary amount of time and effort. So it's, you know, deploying multiple aircraft with customers, flying them, learning, retrofitting, fixing, re-engineering over and over and over again until you achieve, you know, I think the equivalent of product-market fit is a tool that a customer can actually rely on.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, product reliability- market product reliability fit maybe we could call it.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Why are we not using more of your ag drones here in the states? You talked about, you know, Latin America, remote regions. I know that agriculture is different across different areas based on what we're growing and seasons and so forth. But, you know, I've, I've laid drip tape on a farm. I've driven large pieces of equipment. And to me, like going above the crops here would make tons of sense. And your system seems to be very robust and it's got a good spray width and why can't I use this in Nebraska or why aren't people?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, so it's coming. It's coming fast. So the things that drew us to Latin America were twofold. One is regulatory. Um, so Brazil actually deregulated agricultural drones about two years ago, which was very convenient for us. Um, we do have commercial approval to operate aircraft here in the United States. We actually have the largest drone approved for commercial use by the FAA, but it has some cumbersome limitations. We can only fly the drone about four kilometers away from where it took off and landed from, which is not commercially viable today.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No, that's not, that's like, that's...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: It makes sense. Why?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: It has to do with, like, line of sight to the vehicle and—

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Oh, Zipline told me about this, that if you want to fly outside of line of sight, there's another entire set of regulations on top of that. But it works in Brazil, right? So, why?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: It does, yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Is the air different in Brazil compared to the United States? Does something change to navigation and computing when you cross the border?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Actually, it's a totally diff— No, no, it's not. Yeah, so in Brazil we're operating typically anywhere from 5 to 15 kilometers from where we took off and landed from.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: So, I mean, the good news is we have data about this. Like, no one has ever done an operation like this before. And so the FAA understandably is sort of apprehensive about what that looks like. Um, we've been working with them over the last year to actually remove that limitation and expand it to more like what we're doing in Brazil, making really good progress on that. So like I said, we're coming to the US. We do have one customer already here, who's— who has an aircraft, but we're going to have far more in the coming year.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Alright, so I think this is good then to talk about fuel because it's going to come up when we go over to drop-ship in a minute. You currently make mostly electric drones. And I know that when you sell the ag-drone, you send it with multiple sets of batteries so you can kind of hot-swap them and move them in and out. But if I'm six hours from a town, what I probably don't have is as good of access to the grid. So to me, it's lovely that I don't have to cart fuel in a truck up some mountain roads, but I'm curious about just recharging these damn things out in the woods or mountains.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah, so farms have electricity. Like, uh, you know, some farms are pivot irrigated, for example. They have these giant, like, circular pivots that go around, big water pumps, blah, blah, blah. Um, so the farm will always have electricity. Um, the question is whether they want to run the electricity from the sort of center point to the actual runway. Um, and I would say half of the customers that we have today do that. The other half use a diesel generator.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Um, but what's really remarkable, so the— the kind of competition for our aircraft is a vehicle called the Air Tractor. Um, it's a— it's a big, you know, roughly 8,000-pound vehicle. Um, it burns roughly 55 gallons of jet fuel per hour.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Which is not small at today's prices.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: No, yeah, it's a lot. Um, our— the Pelican in the worst case, if you're charging it off of a diesel generator, is about two gallons per hour.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: That's a lot better. Even if you're doing dirty charging, as you might call it, it's still much more efficient. Okay. Now, I know you guys say on the site that the current ag-drone starts at, if memory serves, $550,000. Um, as far as farm equipment goes, that's not super crazy, but it's also not that cheap. So I'm curious about the— the ROI here and kind of like time to— to repay the purchase cost. Because the way that I think about it, more efficient flight, uh, probably need fewer human pilots because it's autonomous and you're using electricity and blah blah blah. So I presume there's some savings baked into this. What is the time to— uh, recovery for people that are changing over to this type of ag work?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's a really good question. So it's, you know, depends highly on how heavily you utilize the vehicle. Um so that's sort of one other benefit of Brazil actually is they're just crazy about the way they do agriculture. They have multiple seasons back to back and they just have an intense culture of like work. Uh so you know, we have customers who, one of our newest customers planning on running three shifts with the Pelican. And so the thing at most could operate literally 24 hours in one day.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: How comfortable would that make you as the guy who has to take the phone call if it goes down while it's working 24 hours a day? Because that sounds like a maintenance nightmare, but maybe I'm overestimating.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: It's intense, but I mean we're already, we have customers doing 12 hours a day right now. So the difference between 12 and 24 is, it's not that big.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: In time are we going to see like smaller farmers be able to do ride-share equivalent of rental for these things to handle their crops? Because I don't think everyone's going to need their own personally.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, correct. Yeah, so in the US for example, you would need to have a farm that is uh almost 20,000 acres in order to utilize, to fully utilize the aircraft. And that's huge. That is a by US standards very, very big. By Brazil standards that's actually like mid-sized. Um so yes, in the US it'll be much more common to have spray-as-a-service where uh you know a contractor owns the vehicle and then services a number of customers in a surrounding radius.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You know we could make an acronym out of that, we could call it SAS. No one's ever used that before.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, totally. Um yeah, maybe our valuation would go up.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Totally.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Anyways, so the uh you asked about economics and payback period. So it yeah depends on the utilization, you know two to three years roughly. But the thing that people don't understand is uh Pelican yes it is a lower cost solution, yes there is a different type of labor you use, a much easier to access labor pool. You can train someone how to operate a Pelican in two to four weeks versus a aerial application pilot is you know 18 months. But really the like the reason people love the Pelican is because of its ability to spray really, really well. Um and so the simplest way to understand this is the cost of the chemical that is being sprayed is typically about four times the cost of the application. So this is this very precious resource.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and you're trying to like deposit this fine mist of chemical very evenly over an entire crop including around the boundaries of the crop which is very difficult. So the Pelican has like a bunch of things about it that make it just a like massively superior solution for actually applying the chemical.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And right now, I mean as you and I record this, the Strait of Hormuz is still totally blocked off and I presume by the time this comes out it'll still be blocked, which is killing fertilizer prices around the world. So people are probably extra price conscious right now about these chemicals they're spraying.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, totally, so input costs are, you know, like chemicals are a very, very significant portion of growing food.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Man. That's, that's...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's, it's worrying to me that the solution to that, which is apparently you guys, is not as applied as the need for fertilizer is, because there's a mismatch between solution and crisis in our food supply. Anyways, for everyone listening to this, if you're confused why we're talking about farming here on TWIST, it's because I'm working this towards a point, which is that Pyka has put together a, not just drone, but also software and the integration with the hardware to make a cool system that allows the company to really quickly build new devices. And this brings into play Dropship, which, as you explained to me before the show, is really the second generation of your cargo plane. So, how did you get Dropship up and into the air so quickly? What have you learned? And then when will it reach the market?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, great question. So, just as general background, Dropship is a dual-use product. Within the commercial sector, it's used for logistics. Within the defense sector, it sort of forks yet again. It is used for contested logistics and then it's also a very versatile multi-mission aircraft, good for carrying large sensor payloads, etc.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Could it carry bombs in the little container? Sorry to be rude, but I'm curious.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: I mean, technically it could. That's not the most, like, interesting use case for it, I would say.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, and why, why not? Because when I think about drones today, we think a lot about, you know, drone-based warfare in Ukraine and in and around the Middle East. And having a large drone that can carry more boom sounds good to me.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so our vehicles are designed to be quite reliable and low operating cost. And so, like, if you want to deliver, if you want to do more kinetic stuff, you don't build a vehicle like we did. Our vehicles last too long, basically. They're too nice. If you wanted to make a really low-cost bomber, there would be different decisions that you'd make than what we did with Dropship.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And probably also like, you know, electronic emissions and noise and there's probably other factors that would have gone into making this a bomber if you wanted it to be one.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Correct. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So you guys had a test flight recently of Dropship. Really quick process to get it, you know, from, as you said, CAD to the runway. Talk me through that.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so we went from, like, initial CAD renderings to first flight in 180 days, which is pretty awesome.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, dude.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: That was, so that wasn't the fastest. I guess what we call Big Bird, the plane that we built in my parents' backyard during Y Combinator technically was faster. But Dropship is way more complicated and useful. So, yeah. It's, you know, it's the second generation cargo plane. First generation cargo plane was an all-electric cargo aircraft. Really cool plane. We built eight of them. We're actually going to probably build quite a few more for commercial customers. That aircraft only...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: The 200-mile useful range, um, so that was a sort of key limitation for defense. Anyways, kind of the backstory there is we built eight of those. Um, we sent three of them to the Air Force, got very positive feedback from them. Um, sent one to the Army, got really positive feedback as well and invitations to some big, uh, demos that are coming up in the next two and a half months. Air Force, we want a follow-on contract as well. Um, I think the thing that was so cool about this is, uh, we got really, really good insights into what it is that both Air Force, SOCOM, and Army want from a, a tradable, contested logistics and multi-mission UAS. So there's no, like, requirements written for this type of vehicle yet. This is too new. Um, and so the main feedback that we got, uh, willing to share it now because Dropship is out in the market, uh, is basically like, Pelican Cargo, the electric thing, really awesome, super easy to use, uh, practical, easy to maintain, needs much longer range. Um, so ideally, 1,000 miles of range with 500 pounds of payload. Check. Dropship hits that. Um, it needs to fit in a 20-foot shipping container. So all of our vehicles actually fit in a 40-foot container already. We designed them to do that, but they're just like two and a half feet too long to go in a 20. Um, so Dropship meets that requirement. The tail is removable, which is really cool.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Ah, okay, got it. Because the first cargo plane opened up in the back like a C-130 versus opening up in the middle like, sorry for the analogy, the bomb bay of a B-17.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, exactly. So the nose opens on both of them, but on the electric one, the whole floor was a battery. Um, so there's no nose—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The nose! The nose opens up, not the back. Oh, I totally misread that image. I'm so sorry, everybody.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: No, no worries. Yeah, the nose opens on both. But yeah, we had this giant battery, uh, in the way, so we, you know, couldn't make the electric one air drop.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Which is I feel we're beating around the bush, the new dropship is hybrid. So it has both a diesel engine as far as I understand it for getting somewhere, and then it has batteries for very quiet looping in and around the area once it arrives.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, exactly. So it's a really cool architecture. Uh, I don't think there's any airplane out there that's a hybrid turbo diesel architecture. Um, so it's a parallel hybrid like you said, so the diesel engine actually has a propeller attached to it. Um, it can also charge the batteries in flight. And then the electric propulsion system... so the diesel engine's behind the fuselage, it's a pusher propeller, um, kind of normal UAV configuration in that sense. And then up in front of the wings are two very high-powered electric motors. Um, and so those are used during takeoff. That's how we get this really ballistic takeoff performance, like takeoff and landing in under 600 feet.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Oh, so you run all three at the same time?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah, absolutely.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, so it just must be going mad like a little bee, zzz, up into the sky.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it sounds really cool. It's this combination of turbo diesel and then the electric. Um, so it's a really cool architecture. I don't think there's any airplane out there that's a hybrid turbo diesel architecture. Um, so it's a parallel hybrid like you said, so the diesel engine actually has a propeller attached to it. Um, it can also charge the batteries in flight.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: So yeah, the diesel engine is about, it's like just over 30 kilowatts peak.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: And then the electric motors in the front are each about 25 kilowatts. So when the electric propulsion system is running, our peak power is essentially three times our cruise power.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: So we use that for this like ballistic takeoff and landing performance, use it to climb to altitude, and then once at altitude, actually shut down the entire electric propulsion system. We have these neat passive folding propellers and then the airplane cruises just on the diesel.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You know, I was really excited about Dropship, not because of its dual-use capabilities, but more because I was thinking about domestic commercial applications, like getting stuff places. Like, can I send my mom a couch? You know? But it sounds like our prior point that we talked about with your agricultural drones is that regulations may not be ready yet to turn Dropship into a domestic FedEx, if you will. Are we going to shoot our own foot here as a nation and not end up with a lot more autonomous flight? Because it seems much more economically viable, environmentally friendly, convenient. Like, it just seems better to me.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, 100%. So I mean, the reason that we kind of pivoted away from mass manufacturing Pelican Cargo, the electric one, wasn't because there was a lack of interest from customers. It was really exactly the reason you mentioned. We could not get regulatory approval to fly at scale beyond visual line of sight operation in a timeline that was relevant to us.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: So that was the biggest factor. Are we shooting ourselves in the foot? Yes, absolutely. I mean, I think there's this very, just getting back to the original point, like these hardware-software blended products are so important to our society. They're also going to create so much value, but their value is 100% determined by their exposure to the real world.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: SpaceX is worth how much it is because they blew up rockets, figured out how to stop doing that, and now the idea of a self-launching, recovering rocket is just like taken for granted. There are very few companies who have figured out a way to actually collect that data. So Pyka, we've done it, we had to go to Brazil. Zipline, they've done it, they had to go to Rwanda.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Zipline, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yep. And then there's a bunch of other companies that have gone to Ukraine. So Ukraine is the other place to like learn, essentially. But outside of that, there are very, very few options, which is troubling.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I want to make sure we get to supply chains, components, and kind of sovereignty. Now, if you were just making agricultural drones for Brazil, I wouldn't really care, but we are talking about the military, we are talking about dual-use a little bit. So how well are you able to source materials, components, or any sort of input without reaching into Chinese supply chains? And how much better can you do in a couple years?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: So, I think it kind of because of when we started the company there wasn't like a bunch of stuff we could just buy off the shelf to build these drones out of. And so we actually vertically integrated essentially every critical component on our product. The motors, batteries, motor controllers are our own design. All the avionics is our own design, airframes, etc., etc., etc. So we're already able to create NDA compliant versions of our product. There is some difference in sourcing. You know, for example, the battery management system we will manufacture here in the United States for an NDA product. For commercial products we'll manufacture the battery management system in China.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, good question. So...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What's the cost differential there?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Uh, it's about 2x.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That's is that a high price component compared to the overall cost of the device?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: No, not I mean so for drop ship, no. There's two BMS's in the entire vehicle. For Pelican, it's more. There's 15 batteries that ship with each airplane.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. So more complicated. Okay, that makes sense to me. I was just thinking that 2x didn't sound as bad as I was expecting and I wasn't quite sure why.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Well, so if you own the design, it's not that bad. I mean, so you can for example, if we don't source from China, we could source from another low cost region. The 2x is actually doing it in the United States though.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, no way. Oh. Huh. All right, well that's better than I thought. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean I think if you own the design and manufacturing in the US versus China is order of magnitude 2x difference. If you don't own the design and you're buying from an OEM and it's like OEM in the US versus OEM in China, then it's probably going to be like 4x difference.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, quite a lot. Okay, that makes perfect sense to me. But you mentioned how when you started the company, things weren't available on the shelf, so you managed to do it all yourself. Did that slow you down materially? And I ask that because it doesn't feel like the company's moving slowly, but you've taken on a lot more what we might call technical risk by building so much in-house that I'm kind of shocked that you've gotten this far given that you really blank sheet of papered this entire thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, for sure. So I mean, I think we have an extremely strong technical team. It has slowed us down. There are some components that have matured really nicely in the sort of OEM space that, you know, if we knew now how far they would have come, we maybe would have paused on developing our own. So the motor controller, for example, was something we did in-house. They've gotten bigger and bigger and better and now there's off the shelf options that are vaguely similar. But you know on the flip side, it's really hard to say because it's kind of the grass is greener scenario. Like, we've run into issues with our own designs and our own products, you know, lots of issues. We've resolved them all.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Or...

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: We're working on the last couple of resolutions, everybody. Give us two, three weeks. Yeah, yeah, exactly. Whereas, you know, we've had issues with our off the shelf products and getting to a robust resolution with those often is far more time consuming. So, yeah, it's hard to say.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: It's tough to say. I think the the biggest thing for us though is like, you know if you think of the most successful hardware companies out there are a blend of hardware software.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Apple.

full_transcript.speaker: Hon Weng Chong
full_transcript.text: Tesla.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Apple, great example. Yeah, Tesla. DJI, perfect example.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Sure. Absolutely.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: And I think like what those companies are able to do is make something incredibly complicated seem just really, really simple to the end user. You know, when you take a picture with your iPhone, it's phenomenally good. It's like better than my dad's super fancy DSLR camera now. And I don't know how or why, but like there's so much going on. There's software...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: What are all these? What are all these little like camera things back here? I don't know what a single one of those does. Not once have I picked it up.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Me neither.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And you know what? My pictures of my kids look fantastic, so thank you Apple.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Totally. Yeah, and so like the way you do that is if you you have to own everything. You have to own the hardware and the software in my opinion. Like that's how you make that really magical experience. If you try and integrate a whole bunch of disparate systems, you end up with this like customer experience where you can tell. Um, and so so that's I think that's like the biggest benefit, but it's it's...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Are we just talking around Boeing's decision to stop making things in-house and just supply everything externally and integrate all these systems? Because I feel like we're slowly starting to circle the Boeing story here. Um, Michael, I I have to let you go, but one last question before I do, which is really simple. There is a lot of money flowing around the venture capital world today. However, I talk to a lot of people that are now building enterprise agent orchestration MCP servers and they tell me that oh my god, people are not interested in us. So does the company have enough access to capital today to continue growing if you still need more money to bring this vision to reality? Because I can see a really cool future in which we have tons of quiet, safe, friendly drones in our skies doing quite a lot of work for us, and I want to get there quickly.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, good question. Um, I would say yes and no. I mean, we've we've been able to raise the money that we need. Uh, if we had more money, we would move faster. Um, so...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: If you're a VC listening to this, hello! Come on! Stop backing SaaS companies that are slapping on an AI wrapper. Back something cool. We're building drones, y'all.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Michael, a treat. Um, where can people find the company and is there a job you are hiring for you want to shout out into the void?

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Yeah, great. Um, so you can find us on our website flypyka.com.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: P-Y-K-A, not P-I-K-A. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Correct, P-Y-K-A. Very active on LinkedIn, actually very active on Instagram. Uh, most of it's in Portuguese, I'll warn you, um, but really, really cool shots from from Brazil of our actual customer operations there.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Fantastic. All right. Thank you very much. We'll see you soon.

full_transcript.speaker: Michael Norcia
full_transcript.text: Great. Thank you.

highlights: Biological computing is demonstrating massive efficiency gains over traditional hardware, with research indicating that human neurons can be 5,000 times more cellularly efficient than GPUs for reinforcement learning tasks.

Cortical Labs has transitioned biological computing from a laboratory curiosity to a commercial reality by building the world’s first biological data centers, which use rack-mountable life-support systems to maintain millions of neurons fused with silicon chips.

A critical ethical "red line" in synthetic biology is the avoidance of consciousness; developers aim to create intelligent systems that lack the capacity to suffer, a boundary established through proactive engagement with bioethicists and religious institutions like the Vatican.

In the field of autonomous aviation, Pyka has deployed the world’s largest commercial drones for agriculture and cargo, utilizing a hybrid turbo-diesel and electric architecture to achieve a 1,000-mile range with significantly lower fuel costs than traditional aircraft.

Innovation in highly regulated sectors is increasingly shifting to international markets, as companies move operations to countries like Brazil and Rwanda to bypass domestic restrictions on "beyond visual line of sight" flight and experimental biological interfaces.
summary:
This transcript features two interviews with founders pushing the boundaries of hardware and specialized computing: Hon Weng Chong of Cortical Labs and Michael Norcia of Pyka. The first segment focuses on Cortical Labs’ development of "biological computers" that fuse silicon chips with human neurons. Their flagship product, the CL1, is a rack-mountable system that sustains living neurons through an integrated life-support system. Beyond drug discovery and medical research, these biological systems have shown remarkable potential in reinforcement learning, proving to be 5,000 times more cellularly efficient than traditional GPU-based systems. The company has launched the world’s first biological data center in Melbourne and is expanding to Singapore, offering a "biological cloud" that allows developers to program these systems using standard tools like Python and Jupyter Notebooks.

The second segment highlights Pyka’s advancements in large-scale autonomous aviation. CEO Michael Norcia discusses their transition from the "Pelican"—an all-electric, autonomous crop-spraying drone widely deployed in Brazil—to "Dropship," a hybrid turbo-diesel cargo drone designed for both commercial logistics and defense. Dropship addresses the range limitations of electric flight, offering a 1,000-mile range while maintaining the ability to take off and land in under 600 feet. A major theme in this discussion is the regulatory friction in the United States; while Pyka's technology is commercially proven in the remote regions of Brazil, FAA line-of-sight restrictions currently limit its domestic viability.

Overall, the transcript explores the theme of vertical integration as a necessity for breakthrough innovation. Both companies have had to build nearly every component of their systems in-house—from life-support pumps for neurons to proprietary motors and flight control software for drones—to achieve the reliability required for commercial use. Whether through biological neurons or autonomous hybrid flight, both startups represent a shift toward high-efficiency, specialized hardware designed to solve complex real-world problems in medicine, logistics, and resource management.
